arguments.prompt: 2D thresholding for segmenting objects based on intensity

response: Will perform 3-levels thresholding based on two thresholds. The low threshold will determine background (for values below), the high threshold the objects cores (for values above) and an intermediate level for values between low and high threshold.

arguments.prompt: measuring features of 2D segments

response: Converts mask images into ROI by applying a connected components algorithm and generates measurements. Please note that this algorithm will always trace the external object boundaries and convert them into polygonal ROIs. This means that holes will be closed. This node requires a thresholded image as input and will extract measurements from the thresholded image. If you already have ROI available and want measurements, use 'Extract ROI statistics'.If higher-dimensional data is provided, the results are generated for each 2D slice.

arguments.prompt: track annotated 2D/3D data across frames by grouping incoming data based on annotations

response:
This tracker is a simple extension of the Intersection - over - Union (IoU) tracker.

It generates links between spots whose shapes overlap between consecutive frames. When several spots are eligible as a source for a target, the one with the largest IoU is chosen.

The minimal IoU parameter sets a threshold below which links won't be created. The scale factor allows for enlarging (>1) or shrinking (<1) the spot shapes before computing their IoU. Two methods can be used to compute IoU: The Fast one approximates the spot shapes by their rectangular bounding-box. The Precise one uses the actual spot polygon.

This tracker works in 2D and 3D. However in 3D, the IoU is computed from the bounding-boxes regardless of the choice of the IoU computation method. The Precise method is not implemented.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.

arguments.prompt: link detected 3D objects across consecutive frames

response:
This tracker is a simple extension of the Intersection - over - Union (IoU) tracker.

It generates links between spots whose shapes overlap between consecutive frames. When several spots are eligible as a source for a target, the one with the largest IoU is chosen.

The minimal IoU parameter sets a threshold below which links won't be created. The scale factor allows for enlarging (>1) or shrinking (<1) the spot shapes before computing their IoU. Two methods can be used to compute IoU: The Fast one approximates the spot shapes by their rectangular bounding-box. The Precise one uses the actual spot polygon.

This tracker works in 2D and 3D. However in 3D, the IoU is computed from the bounding-boxes regardless of the choice of the IoU computation method. The Precise method is not implemented.

arguments.prompt: Compute colocalization metrics between two markers

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

The description of the node returned by this tool call should confirm its capability to perform the necessary 2D-to-3D conversion for morphological analysis, aligning with the overall goal of creating a pipeline that segments cells and measures their morphological features effectively.

arguments.prompt: convert 2D cell segmentations to 3D volumes

response:
Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: separate touching cells after segmentation

response:
Runs Cellpose on the input image with the given model(s). This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

arguments.prompt: segment cells based on intensity

response:
Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value. Please note that ImageJ will run into issues if too many objects are present and the labels are saved in int32. ImageJ would load them as float32.
  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

arguments.prompt: apply sharpening filter based on blur measurement

response: Subtracts a blurred copy of the image and rescales the image to obtain the same contrast of large (low-frequency) structures as in the input image. This is equivalent to adding a high-pass filtered image and thus sharpens the image. "Radius (Sigma)" is the standard deviation (blur radius) of the Gaussian blur that is subtracted. "Mask Weight" determines the strength of filtering, where "Mask Weight"=1 would be an infinite weight of the high-pass filtered image that is added. If higher-dimensional data is provided, the filter is applied to each 2D slice.

6b7610d5-9e7a-4a8a-8b4f-07633b4f3ff1.final_output:
To address your request for creating a JIPipe pipeline that corrects for uneven illumination and motion blur in a stack of images acquired from a microscope, followed by cell segmentation, I will outline the main steps this pipeline will take. 

Firstly, the pipeline will focus on correcting for uneven illumination. This step is crucial because it directly impacts the quality of subsequent processing steps, including cell segmentation. The goal here is to ensure that each pixel within the image stack receives an appropriate illumination value, which can be done through various methods such as flat-field correction or using a reference image for normalization.

Following the illumination correction, the pipeline will then aim to reduce motion blur. Motion blur is a common issue in microscopy images, especially when the sample is moving during image acquisition. By reducing this blur, the pipeline can enhance the overall image quality, making it more suitable for subsequent processing steps like cell segmentation. Techniques for motion blur reduction include deconvolution methods or using algorithms specifically designed to remove motion artifacts from images.

After these initial steps of illumination correction and motion blur reduction, the pipeline will then proceed to perform cell segmentation on the corrected images. This step is critical as it directly addresses your main goal of segmenting cells within the image stack. The choice of cell segmentation algorithm can vary depending on the specific needs of your project, such as whether you're working with 2D or 3D images, the type of cells being analyzed, and any specific requirements for output data formats.

In summary, the main steps the JIPipe pipeline will take to achieve your goals include:
1. Correcting for uneven illumination to ensure each pixel receives appropriate illumination values.
2. Reducing motion blur to enhance image quality and remove artifacts.
3. Performing cell segmentation on the corrected images, potentially utilizing advanced algorithms capable of handling complex cell morphologies and outputs.

By following this structured approach, the pipeline aims to provide you with a robust and effective method for segmenting cells within your microscopy image stack, addressing both illumination and motion blur issues along the way.

Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value. Please note that ImageJ will run into issues if too many objects are present and the labels are saved in int32. ImageJ would load them as float32.
  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

arguments.prompt: compute particle coordinates

response:
This tracker is based on the Linear Assignment Problem mathematical framework.
Its implementation is adapted from the following paper:
Robust single-particle tracking in live-cell time-lapse sequences -
Jaqaman et al., 2008, Nature Methods.

Tracking happens in 2 steps: First spots are linked from frame to frame to
build track segments. These track segments are investigated in a second step
for gap-closing (missing detection), splitting and merging events.

Linking costs are proportional to the square distance between source and
target spots, which makes this tracker suitable for Brownian motion.
Penalties can be set to favor linking between spots that have similar
features.

Solving the LAP relies on the Jonker-Volgenant solver, and a sparse cost matrix formulation, allowing it to handle very large problems.

090aa7ed-e9b7-4cdb-912c-bacdc5bd9416.final_output:
To address your requirement for quantifying the spatial distribution of fluorescent particles in images, I propose creating a JIPipe pipeline that focuses on detecting these particles and then calculating their spatial coordinates. The main steps this pipeline will take include identifying the particles within your images, tracking their movements over time (if applicable), and ultimately generating a heat map visualization that represents their density and distribution across the image.

The pipeline will start by detecting the fluorescent particles in your images. This detection process is crucial as it directly influences all subsequent steps, ensuring that we are working with accurate and relevant data. Once particles are identified, the pipeline will consider tracking these particles over time if they are moving. This tracking aspect is optional but can be beneficial for analyzing particle dynamics or behavior.

Following particle detection and potentially tracking, the pipeline will focus on calculating the spatial coordinates of these particles. This step is essential for understanding how particles are distributed across the image and for generating an accurate heat map. The method used for calculating these coordinates could involve algorithms like 2D tracking, which aligns with your goal of visualizing particle density.

The final step involves generating a heat map that visually represents the density and distribution of fluorescent particles across your image. This heat map is a key output of the pipeline, providing a comprehensive overview of how particles are distributed and their relative density in different regions of the image.

In summary, the main steps the pipeline will take include:
1. Fluorescent particle detection.
2. (Optional) Tracking of moving particles.
3. Calculation of particle spatial coordinates.
4. Generation of a heat map visualizing particle density and distribution.

This approach ensures that we are systematically building towards your desired outcome: a clear and meaningful representation of fluorescent particle distribution in your images. By following these steps, the pipeline will effectively quantify the spatial distribution of fluorescent particles, providing valuable insights into their behavior and density across your image dataset.

arguments.prompt: track cells across frames

response:
This tracker is the most simple one, and is based on nearest neighbor
search. The spots in the target frame are searched for the nearest neighbor
of each spot in the source frame. If the spots found are closer than the
maximal allowed distance, a link between the two is created.

The nearest neighbor search relies upon the KD-tree technique implemented
in imglib by Johannes Schindelin and friends. This ensure a very efficient tracking and makes this tracker suitable for situation where a huge number
of particles are to be tracked over a very large number of frames. However,
because of the naiveness of its principles, it can result in pathological
tracks. It can only do frame-to-frame linking; there cannot be any track
merging or splitting, and gaps will not be closed. Also, the end results are non-deterministic.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.

  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)

  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.

ba13c193-109d-4cf1-b9c7-45b2a881a648.prompt: I want to measure the distance between two distinct sets of objects in an image, for example, the distance between cell nuclei and cell edges. Can you create a JIPipe pipeline that segments both types of objects, and then calculates the distance between each nucleus and its nearest edge, outputting these distances as a table?

model_message.thinking:
To further develop the pipeline according to the user's requirements, we need to focus on the next crucial step after identifying and segmenting the cell nuclei and cell edges. Given that our goal is to calculate the distance between each nucleus and its nearest edge, and considering the previous steps have already included nodes for segmenting these objects, our next logical step involves connecting these segments to a tool that can calculate distances between points or sets of points.

Reflecting on the current state of the pipeline, which includes nodes for segmenting cell nuclei and cell edges, we now aim to link these outputs to a node that can perform the desired distance calculation. To achieve this, we should utilize a tool that can provide us with the name of a node capable of calculating distances between points, given that our input is a list of points (in this case, the segmented nuclei and edges).

The reasoning leads us to prepare a prompt for the 'get_node_name' tool that describes the functionality of calculating distances between sets of points, with an emphasis on being able to handle lists of points as input. This prompt should be concise yet informative, allowing the tool to identify the most suitable node for our needs.

Upon receiving the response from the 'get_node_name' tool, which should provide us with a node name and its description, we will assess whether this node indeed matches our requirement for calculating distances between nuclei and edges. If it does, we can then proceed to add this node to our pipeline, ensuring it is connected appropriately to the existing nodes for segmenting cell nuclei and cell edges.

Therefore, the next step involves preparing an appropriate prompt for the 'get_node_name' tool, which should include something like "calculate distance between sets of points" or "find node that calculates distance between lists of points," thereby aiming to identify and potentially include a node that calculates distances between points or sets of points, which aligns with our overall goal of calculating distances between cell nuclei and their nearest edges.

arguments.prompt: find node that calculates distance between lists of points

response:
Computes the distance between all point coordinates given in two point lists.

Takes two images containing pointlists (dimensionality n * d, n: number of points and d: dimensionality) and builds up a matrix containing the distances between these points.

Convention: Given two point lists with dimensionality n * d and m * d, the distance matrix will be of size(n + 1) * (m + 1). The first row and column contain zeros. They represent the distance of the objects to a theoretical background object. In that way, distance matrices are of the same size as touch matrices (see generateTouchMatrix). Thus, one can threshold a distance matrix to generate a touch matrix out of it for drawing meshes.

Parameters: Image coordinate_list1, Image coordinate_list2, ByRef Image distance_matrix_destination
Available for: 2D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

arguments.prompt: Apply object detection algorithm to 2D images

response:
Template matching is an algorithm that can be used for object-detections in grayscale images. To perform template matching you will need a template image that is searched in the target image.
The best is to simply crop a typical region of interest from a representative image.



The algorithm computes the probability to find one (or several) template images provided by the user into a larger image. The algorithm uses the template image as a sliding window translated over the image, and at each position of the template computes a similarity score between the template and the image patch.
This results in a correlation map, for which the pixel value at position (x,y) is proportional to the similarity between the template and image patch, at this (x,y) position in the image. The computation of the correlation map is followed by extrema detection to list the possible locations of the template in the image. Each extrema corresponds to the possible location of a bounding box of dimensions identical to the template. The extrema detection can list a large number of extrema in the first place. Usually a threshold on the score is then used to limit this number (i.e. returning local extrema with a score above/below the threshold).
To prevent overlapping detection of the same object, Non-Maxima Supression is performed after extrema detection.



Please visit https://github.com/multi-template-matching/MultiTemplateMatching-Fiji/wiki for more information about the Multi-Template Matching plugin.

Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

The key components this pipeline will focus on include annotating cells with their corresponding frames to enable accurate tracking across time, identifying cells that are dividing by analyzing their annotations and potentially applying algorithms like Cellpose for robust segmentation, and then calculating the time intervals between these initial signs of division and the complete separation of daughter cells.

The output of these nodes will be critical in ensuring that subsequent steps in the pipeline, such as tracking and measurement, are properly informed and can accurately calculate division times.

In summary, the main steps this pipeline aims to take are:
1. Annotate cells with their corresponding frames to enable accurate tracking across time.
2. Identify cells that are dividing by analyzing their annotations and applying appropriate algorithms for robust segmentation.
3. Calculate the time intervals between the first signs of division and the complete separation of daughter cells based on annotated data.
4. Output a histogram showing the distribution of division times.

This approach aligns with your requirements for analyzing time-lapse microscopy videos of cell division and aims to provide a comprehensive pipeline for quantifying division times and their distribution, which is crucial for various biological and medical research applications.

arguments.prompt: track particles across frames based on their 2D positions

response:
This tracker is based on the Linear Assignment Problem mathematical framework.
Its implementation is adapted from the following paper:
Robust single-particle tracking in live-cell time-lapse sequences -
Jaqaman et al., 2008, Nature Methods.

Tracking happens in 2 steps: First spots are linked from frame to frame to
build track segments. These track segments are investigated in a second step
for gap-closing (missing detection), splitting and merging events.

Linking costs are proportional to the square distance between source and
target spots, which makes this tracker suitable for Brownian motion.
Penalties can be set to favor linking between spots that have similar
features.

Solving the LAP relies on the Jonker-Volgenant solver, and a sparse cost matrix formulation, allowing it to handle very large problems.

arguments.prompt: track cells across frames by assigning unique identifiers

response:
This tracker is the most simple one, and is based on nearest neighbor
search. The spots in the target frame are searched for the nearest neighbor
of each spot in the source frame. If the spots found are closer than the
maximal allowed distance, a link between the two is created.

The nearest neighbor search relies upon the KD-tree technique implemented
in imglib by Johannes Schindelin and friends. This ensure a very efficient tracking and makes this tracker suitable for situation where a huge number
of particles are to be tracked over a very large number of frames. However,
because of the naiveness of its principles, it can result in pathological
tracks. It can only do frame-to-frame linking; there cannot be any track
merging or splitting, and gaps will not be closed. Also, the end results are non-deterministic.

arguments.prompt: calculate distance between points in 2D or 3D space

response:
Computes the distance between all point coordinates given in two point lists.

Takes two images containing pointlists (dimensionality n * d, n: number of points and d: dimensionality) and builds up a matrix containing the distances between these points.

Convention: Given two point lists with dimensionality n * d and m * d, the distance matrix will be of size(n + 1) * (m + 1). The first row and column contain zeros. They represent the distance of the objects to a theoretical background object. In that way, distance matrices are of the same size as touch matrices (see generateTouchMatrix). Thus, one can threshold a distance matrix to generate a touch matrix out of it for drawing meshes.

Parameters: Image coordinate_list1, Image coordinate_list2, ByRef Image distance_matrix_destination
Available for: 2D

arguments.prompt: calculate displacement vectors between consecutive frames for tracked cells

response:
Transfers image sequences into an optic flow field.
Flow fields are calculated for each pair (t,t+1) of the sequence with length |T| independently. The motion vector for each pixel in image t is estimated by searching the most similar looking pixel in image t+1. The similarity measure is Pearson Product-Moment Correlation Coefficient of all pixels in a local vicinity. The local vicinity is defined by a block and is calculated using an IntegralImage. Both the size of the block and the search radius are parameters of the method.

The output is a two-channel image with (T-1) items. The pixels in each channel describe the relative location of the next similar pixel in polar coordinates (default) or cartesian coordinates.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.

arguments.prompt: calculate cell speed from tracked positions

response:
This tracker is best suited for objects that move with a roughly constant velocity vector.

It relies on the Kalman filter to predict the next most likely position of a spot. The predictions for all current tracks are linked to the spots actually found in the next frame, thanks to the LAP framework already present in the LAP tracker. Predictions are continuously refined and the tracker can accommodate moderate velocity direction and magnitude changes.

This tracker can bridge gaps: If a spot is not found close enough to a prediction, then the Kalman filter will make another prediction in the next frame and re-iterate the search.

The first frames of a track are critical for this tracker to work properly: Tracksare initiated by looking for close neighbors (again via the LAP tracker). Spurious spots in the beginning of each track can confuse the tracker.

This tracker needs two parameters (on top of the maximal frame gap tolerated):
- the max search radius defines how far from a predicted position it should look for candidate spots;
- the initial search radius defines how far two spots can be apart when initiating a new track.

Runs Omnipose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Omnipose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Omnipose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Omnipose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

response:
This tracker is the most simple one, and is based on nearest neighbor
search. The spots in the target frame are searched for the nearest neighbor
of each spot in the source frame. If the spots found are closer than the
maximal allowed distance, a link between the two is created.

The nearest neighbor search relies upon the KD-tree technique implemented
in imglib by Johannes Schindelin and friends. This ensure a very efficient tracking and makes this tracker suitable for situation where a huge number
of particles are to be tracked over a very large number of frames. However,
because of the naiveness of its principles, it can result in pathological
tracks. It can only do frame-to-frame linking; there cannot be any track
merging or splitting, and gaps will not be closed. Also, the end results are non-deterministic.

arguments.prompt: apply denoising filter to enhance signal quality

response:
Applies the bilateral filter to an image.

The function applies bilateral filtering to the input image, as described in http://www.dai.ed.ac.uk/CVonline/LOCAL_COPIES/MANDUCHI1/Bilateral_Filtering.html bilateralFilter can reduce unwanted noise very well while keeping edges fairly sharp. However, it is very slow compared to most filters.

Sigma values: For simplicity, you can set the 2 sigma values to be the same. If they are small (< 10), the filter will not have much effect, whereas if they are large (> 150), they will have a very strong effect, making the image look "cartoonish".

Filter size: Large filters (d > 5) are very slow, so it is recommended to use d=5 for real-time applications, and perhaps d=9 for offline applications that need heavy noise filtering.

arguments.prompt: 2D image co-localization analysis using Manders' coefficient

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

arguments.prompt: 2D grayscale image segmentation

response:
Labeles objects directly from grey-value images.

The two sigma parameters allow tuning the segmentation result. The first sigma controls how close detected cells can be (spot_sigma) and the second controls how precise segmented objects are outlined (outline_sigma).Under the hood, this filter applies two Gaussian blurs, spot detection, Otsu-thresholding and Voronoi-labeling.
The thresholded binary image is flooded using the Voronoi approach starting from the found local maxima.
Noise-removal sigma for spot detection and thresholding can be configured separately.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number spot_sigma, Number outline_sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient between two lists of 2D ROIs

response:
Filters the ROI lists by testing for mutual overlap. The ROI1 output contains all ROI1 input ROI that overlap with any of ROI2. The ROI2 output contains all ROI2 input ROI that overlap with a ROI1 ROI.

arguments.prompt: Apply denoising filter to enhance image quality

response:
Applies the bilateral filter to an image.

The function applies bilateral filtering to the input image, as described in http://www.dai.ed.ac.uk/CVonline/LOCAL_COPIES/MANDUCHI1/Bilateral_Filtering.html bilateralFilter can reduce unwanted noise very well while keeping edges fairly sharp. However, it is very slow compared to most filters.

Sigma values: For simplicity, you can set the 2 sigma values to be the same. If they are small (< 10), the filter will not have much effect, whereas if they are large (> 150), they will have a very strong effect, making the image look "cartoonish".

Filter size: Large filters (d > 5) are very slow, so it is recommended to use d=5 for real-time applications, and perhaps d=9 for offline applications that need heavy noise filtering.

arguments.prompt: Apply denoising filter to enhance image quality

response:
Applies the bilateral filter to an image.

The function applies bilateral filtering to the input image, as described in http://www.dai.ed.ac.uk/CVonline/LOCAL_COPIES/MANDUCHI1/Bilateral_Filtering.html bilateralFilter can reduce unwanted noise very well while keeping edges fairly sharp. However, it is very slow compared to most filters.

Sigma values: For simplicity, you can set the 2 sigma values to be the same. If they are small (< 10), the filter will not have much effect, whereas if they are large (> 150), they will have a very strong effect, making the image look "cartoonish".

Filter size: Large filters (d > 5) are very slow, so it is recommended to use d=5 for real-time applications, and perhaps d=9 for offline applications that need heavy noise filtering.

arguments.prompt: segment image based on greyscale values

response:
Labeles objects directly from grey-value images.

The two sigma parameters allow tuning the segmentation result. The first sigma controls how close detected cells can be (spot_sigma) and the second controls how precise segmented objects are outlined (outline_sigma).Under the hood, this filter applies two Gaussian blurs, spot detection, Otsu-thresholding and Voronoi-labeling.
The thresholded binary image is flooded using the Voronoi approach starting from the found local maxima.
Noise-removal sigma for spot detection and thresholding can be configured separately.

arguments.prompt: apply segmentation algorithm to accurately separate fluorescent protein from background and identify organelle within cell

response:
Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value. Please note that ImageJ will run into issues if too many objects are present and the labels are saved in int32. ImageJ would load them as float32.
  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient based on Omnipose output

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

arguments.prompt: 2D segmentation of 8-bit grayscale images

response:
Labeles objects directly from grey-value images.

The two sigma parameters allow tuning the segmentation result. The first sigma controls how close detected cells can be (spot_sigma) and the second controls how precise segmented objects are outlined (outline_sigma).Under the hood, this filter applies two Gaussian blurs, spot detection, Otsu-thresholding and Voronoi-labeling.
The thresholded binary image is flooded using the Voronoi approach starting from the found local maxima.
Noise-removal sigma for spot detection and thresholding can be configured separately.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number spot_sigma, Number outline_sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D

arguments.prompt: 2D co-localization calculation between two 8-bit grayscale images

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value.
  • Flows XY: An RGB image that indicates the x and y flow of each pixel
  • Flows Z: Flows in Z direction (black for non-3D images)
  • Flows d: Multi-channel image that contains the flows [dZ, dY, dX, cell probability] (3D images) / [dY, dX, cell probability] (2D images)
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the cell probabilities for each pixel
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.

arguments.prompt: Apply denoising filter to improve image quality

response:
Applies the bilateral filter to an image.

The function applies bilateral filtering to the input image, as described in http://www.dai.ed.ac.uk/CVonline/LOCAL_COPIES/MANDUCHI1/Bilateral_Filtering.html bilateralFilter can reduce unwanted noise very well while keeping edges fairly sharp. However, it is very slow compared to most filters.

Sigma values: For simplicity, you can set the 2 sigma values to be the same. If they are small (< 10), the filter will not have much effect, whereas if they are large (> 150), they will have a very strong effect, making the image look "cartoonish".

Filter size: Large filters (d > 5) are very slow, so it is recommended to use d=5 for real-time applications, and perhaps d=9 for offline applications that need heavy noise filtering.

arguments.prompt: 2D or 3D image segmentation for co-localization analysis

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

arguments.prompt: segment objects in image

response:
The node works with two images, one containing the seeds of the objects, that can be obtained from local maxima (see 3D Filters or 3D Maxima Finder), the other image containing signal data. The program computes a local threshold around each seeds and cluster voxels with values higher than the local threshold computed. Three circles are drawn. The user defines the radius (in pixels) of each
circle, given that the first circle should be located within the object, while
the two other circles should be located outside of the object. The mean
intensities of the object (within the first circle), and of the background (in
between the two other circles) are measured and the threshold calculated.
By default, the threshold is the mean of the two mean intensity values
(weight=0.5). The user can however shift this parameter: for a weight
value of 0.75, the threshold will be closer to the background value.

arguments.prompt: Apply 2D anisotropic diffusion denoising filter

response:

Applies a non-local means filter using a box neighborhood with a Gaussian weight specified with sigma to the input image.

Available for: 2D, 3D

arguments.prompt: co-localization analysis

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

The first sigma controls how close detected cells can be (spot_sigma) and the second controls how precise segmented objects are outlined (outline_sigma).Under the hood, this filter applies two Gaussian blurs, spot detection, Otsu-thresholding and Voronoi-labeling.
The thresholded binary image is flooded using the Voronoi approach starting from the found local maxima.
Noise-removal sigma for spot detection and thresholding can be configured separately.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number spot_sigma, Number outline_sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization metric (e.g., Pearson's or Manders') between two labeled images

response:
Compares two label or binary images and calculates measurements of their overlap error or agreement. Measurements include:
  • Overlap
  • Jaccard index
  • Dice coefficient
  • Volume Similarity
  • False Negative Error
  • False Positive Error

arguments.prompt: co-localization analysis of 2D greyscale images

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization between two images

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

arguments.prompt: Apply denoising filter to 2D grayscale image

response: Applies a 3x3 median filter to 8-bit greyscale images. If higher-dimensional data is provided, the filter is applied to each 2D slice.

arguments.prompt: apply denoising filter

response:
Author: Robert Haase, based on work by Loic A. Royer

Applies a non-local means filter using a box neighborhood with a Gaussian weight specified with sigma to the input image.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number radiusX, Number radiusY, Number radiusZ, Number sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

arguments.prompt: 2D or 3D segmentation using a thresholding algorithm

response: Will perform 3-levels thresholding based on two thresholds. The low threshold will determine background (for values below), the high threshold the objects cores (for values above) and an intermediate level for values between low and high threshold.

arguments.prompt: find objects in an image

response:
Template matching is an algorithm that can be used for object-detections in grayscale images. To perform template matching you will need a template image that is searched in the target image.
The best is to simply crop a typical region of interest from a representative image.



The algorithm computes the probability to find one (or several) template images provided by the user into a larger image. The algorithm uses the template image as a sliding window translated over the image, and at each position of the template computes a similarity score between the template and the image patch.
This results in a correlation map, for which the pixel value at position (x,y) is proportional to the similarity between the template and image patch, at this (x,y) position in the image. The computation of the correlation map is followed by extrema detection to list the possible locations of the template in the image. Each extrema corresponds to the possible location of a bounding box of dimensions identical to the template. The extrema detection can list a large number of extrema in the first place. Usually a threshold on the score is then used to limit this number (i.e. returning local extrema with a score above/below the threshold).
To prevent overlapping detection of the same object, Non-Maxima Supression is performed after extrema detection.



Please visit https://github.com/multi-template-matching/MultiTemplateMatching-Fiji/wiki for more information about the Multi-Template Matching plugin.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of multiple data items. If you want to group all data into one output, set the matching strategy to 'Custom' and leave 'Data set matching annotations' empty.

b8b88d84-3d4c-4715-a9f1-1e53a6611399.prompt: I have a time-series of images showing the expression of a fluorescent protein in cells, and I want to quantify the co-localization between this protein and a specific organelle over time. The signal is weak and noisy. Can you design a JIPipe pipeline that first applies denoising filters, then accurately segments both the protein and the organelle in each frame, and finally calculates a co-localization coefficient (like Pearson’s or Manders’) for each cell at each time point, outputting a time-series of co-localization values?

arguments.prompt: apply denoising filter to incoming data

response: Applies a Kuwahara filter, a noise-reduction filter that preserves edges. Also supports the linear Kuwahara algorithm. If higher-dimensional data is provided, the filter is applied to each 2D slice.

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

arguments.prompt: apply denoising filter to image

response:
Author: Robert Haase, based on work by Loic A. Royer

Applies a non-local means filter using a box neighborhood with a Gaussian weight specified with sigma to the input image.

Parameters: Image input, ByRef Image destination, Number radiusX, Number radiusY, Number radiusZ, Number sigma
Available for: 2D, 3D



Please be aware that this node was generated automatically by JIPipe from existing interfaces provided by CLIJ. Please contact the JIPipe developers if you find any issues.

arguments.prompt: 2D image segmentation
3D image segmentation

response:
Performs segmentation via watershed on a 2D or 3D image using flooding simulations. Please note that this node returns labels instead of masks. The markers need to be labels, so apply a connected components labeling if you only have masks.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

Will perform 3-levels thresholding based on two thresholds. The low threshold will determine background (for values below), the high threshold the objects cores (for values above) and an intermediate level for values between low and high threshold.

arguments.prompt: calculate co-localization coefficient between two 2D images

response:
Colocalization analysis via the pixel intensity correlation over space methods of Pearson, Manders, Costes, Li and more, for scatterplots, analysis, automatic thresholding and statistical significance testing. Coloc 2 does NOT perform object based colocalization measurements, where objects are first segmented from the image, then their spatial relationships like overlap etc. are measured. This complementary approach is implemented in many ways elsewhere.

This algorithm groups the incoming data based on the annotations. Those groups can consist of one data item per slot.

arguments.prompt: cell segmentation

response:
Runs Cellpose on the input image. This node supports both segmentation in 3D and executing Cellpose for each 2D image plane. This node can generate a multitude of outputs, although only ROI is activated by default. Go to the 'Outputs' parameter section to enable the other outputs.
  • Labels: A grayscale image where each connected component is assigned a unique value. Please note that ImageJ will run into issues if too many objects are present and the labels are saved in int32. ImageJ would load them as float32.
  • Flows: An RGB image that indicates the flow of each pixel. Convert it to HSB to extract information.
  • Probabilities: An image indicating the probabilities for each pixel.
  • Styles: A vector summarizing each image.
  • ROI: ROI of the segmented areas.
Please note that you need to setup a valid Python environment with Cellpose installed. You can find the setting in Project > Application settings > Extensions > Cellpose.

This algorithm merges all annotations and data annotations. Use the iteration step settings to determine how annotations and data annotations are merged
